questions.question: Define One-Time Pad and state its four essential requirements for achieving perfect secrecy.
keyPoints: One-Time Pad is a symmetric encryption using random key equal to message length
Key must be truly random (not pseudo-random)
Key length must be ≥ plaintext length
Key must be used only once
Key must be kept completely secret

questions.question: Using a binary example, demonstrate how OTP encryption and decryption work with XOR operation.
keyPoints: Encryption: C = P ⊕ K where ⊕ is XOR
Decryption: P = C ⊕ K (self-inverse property)
Example showing specific binary values
Proof that XOR undoes itself: (P⊕K)⊕K = P

questions.question: State and explain Shannon's Perfect Secrecy theorem in the context of One-Time Pad.
keyPoints: Theorem: Perfect secrecy requires |K| ≥ |M|
For OTP, |K| = |M| achieves this bound
H(P|C) = H(P): ciphertext reveals no information
All keys equally likely → attacker cannot distinguish

questions.question: Explain why key reuse is fatal in One-Time Pad with a mathematical analysis.
keyPoints: When same key K used for two messages: C1 = P1⊕K, C2 = P2⊕K
XORing ciphertexts: C1⊕C2 = (P1⊕K)⊕(P2⊕K) = P1⊕P2
Key cancels out due to self-inverse property
Attacker learns relationship between plaintexts
Historical example: USSR spy messages decrypted this way

questions.question: Compare One-Time Pad with Caesar Cipher in terms of security and key space.
keyPoints: Caesar: 25 possible keys; OTP: 2^n keys for n-bit message
Caesar vulnerable to frequency analysis; OTP reveals no statistical patterns
Caesar is breakable; OTP is provably unbreakable
Brute force on Caesar yields唯一 solution; OTP yields all possible messages

questions.question: A company wants to use OTP for securing 1TB backup data. Discuss the practical challenges they would face.
keyPoints: Key distribution: need to securely share 1TB of random key material
Key storage: keys must be stored securely and accessed on demand
Key synchronization: sender and receiver need identical keys
True randomness: cannot use pseudo-random generators
Key management: tracking which keys used/unused at scale

questions.question: Provide a rigorous mathematical proof that One-Time Pad achieves perfect secrecy using information theory concepts.
keyPoints: Define perfect secrecy: Pr[P=p|C=c] = Pr[P=p] for all p,c
Let M be message space, K be key space with uniform distribution
For any ciphertext c = m⊕k for some k∈K
For any plaintext m', there exists key k' = m'⊕c such that m' encrypts to c
All |K| plaintexts equally likely given c
Therefore Pr[P=m|C=c] = 1/|M| = Pr[P=m] → perfect secrecy
Prove H(P|C) = H(P): conditional entropy equals original entropy

questions.question: Given that OTP is theoretically unbreakable, explain why it is rarely used in practice. Discuss alternatives that achieve computational security.
keyPoints: OTP practical issues: key distribution, key storage, key synchronization
Modern alternatives: AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) with 128/256-bit keys
These rely on computational assumptions (P≠NP, no efficient factoring)
Block cipher modes: CBC, CTR provide semantic security
Stream ciphers: ChaCha20 provide practical alternatives
Trade-off: computational vs information-theoretic security

questions.question: An attacker intercepts two OTP-encrypted messages C1 and C2. Given your knowledge that same key was used (violating OTP), explain how you would attempt to recover the plaintexts. What information can you extract and what remains unknown?
keyPoints: Compute C1⊕C2 = P1⊕P2 - reveals relationship between plaintexts
If plaintext has known structure (English text), perform crib-dragging
Statistical analysis of P1⊕P2 reveals patterns in language
Cannot recover absolute plaintexts without additional information
If one plaintext is known, other is immediately revealed
Discuss解决方法: language redundancy, statistical attacks
